text: The surgeon applies two Allis forceps on either side of the umbilicus to avert the crease. An incision is made at the inferior crease of the umbilicus using an #11 blade. A Veress needle is introduced, followed by irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm entry into the abdominal cavity, indicated by the appearance of bubbles.

A few drops of saline are placed on the abdominal wall, which is then lifted, allowing the saline to be sucked in. The surgeon initiates insufflation using a Striker pneumoflator at a flow rate of one liter per minute. As gas enters the abdominal cavity, the liver dendrosis becomes obliterated, transitioning from a double-digit to a single-digit measurement.

The surgeon monitors the insufflation process, observing the end-tidal CO2 concentration at 27 on a multi-parameter monitor, indicating correct placement of CO2. Approximately 3.5 liters of gas is introduced, reaching an intra-abdominal pressure of 13 mmHg. The surgeon uses mosquito forceps to dilate the obliterated vitreous intestinal duct, then holds the trocar and cannula like a pistol, with the middle finger around the air inlet and index finger towards the sharp end, supporting the abdominal wall for introduction using a screwing motion.

Inside the abdomen, a large endometrioma is visible on the right ovary, surrounded by some fluid. The patient has endometriosis, with visible adhesions and sub-serous fibroids. The surgeon uses the harmonic device to separate the outer layer of the ovarian cortex.
The surgeon enlarges the incision on the ovary using harmonic traction, carefully opening only the outer layer of the ovarian cortex to avoid rupturing the endometrioma.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper in the left hand to stabilize the tissue while the vibrating jaw of the harmonic in the right hand splits the outer layer of the ovarian cortex. The endometrioma ruptures, and its contents begin to leak out.

The surgeon uses suction to remove the chocolate-colored fluid from the ruptured endometrioma, preventing spillage. The cyst collapses, facilitating the ovarian cystectomy. The surgeon employs a Maryland dissector and atraumatic grasper to excise the entire cystic wall.

The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand and a Maryland in the right hand to strip the ovarian cyst. The procedure involves alternating between holding the cyst with one hand and stripping the ovarian cortex with the other, aiming to achieve a good plane for complete removal of the thick cystic wall.

The surgeon uses ambidextrous techniques to strip the remaining chocolate material from the ovarian cyst, ensuring no part of the cyst is left behind. The remaining ovary is visible as the cystic wall is carefully separated from the ovarian cortex. Suction is used intermittently to manage any dribbling cystic material. Half of the cyst has been successfully separated at this stage.

The surgeon uses suction irrigation to remove the remaining chocolate material from the cyst, ensuring that the patient's head is not positioned too low to prevent the contents from moving towards the upper abdomen. The suction device is used to find a good plane and effectively evacuate all cystic contents.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector and a semi-traumatic grasper to perform further ovarian cystectomy, carefully separating the ovarian cyst from the ovary. The ovary is handled gently to preserve the cortex material during the stripping process.

The surgeon has removed approximately ninety percent of the cystic wall and is using instruments to separate the remaining portion. The cystic wall tends to slip, requiring careful reorientation and grasping to complete the separation.
The surgeon places the removed ovarian cyst over the anterior cul-de-sac to ensure it is not missed. The entire cystic wall has been successfully stripped out, completing the cystectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs a thorough lavage to remove leaked chocolate material. A small sub-serous myoma, approximately fifteen millimeters in size, is excised. Another myoma, approximately thirty millimeters in size, is identified for excision.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform a simple myoma surgery, carefully separating the fibroid while avoiding damage to the serous layer. Minimal energy application ensures hemostasis, preventing bleeding or oozing as the myoma is successfully separated.

The surgeon uses a stone holding forceps to extract the first myoma, approximately fifteen millimeters in size, followed by the second myoma, held longitudinally to prevent transverse arrest during removal. The small size of these myomas negates the need for morcellation.

The surgeon uses a commercially available endo bag with a metal ring that opens like a basket. A grasper is used to drop all the cystic content into the double-layer polypropylene endo bag, which is strong and resistant to tearing during removal.

The surgeon places the cyst inside the endo bag, which features a metal ring that closes with a purse string effect. The mouth of the endo bag is closed completely, allowing the cyst to be safely extracted using ovum forceps. The endo bag acts as a wound protector, preventing contamination of the port wound during removal.

The surgeon lifts the uterus and places an Interceed barrier over the area where adhesiolysis was performed. The Interceed, a deoxygenated cellulose material, acts as a mechanical barrier to prevent adhesion between the uterus and rectum.

The surgeon spreads the interseed material along the posterior wall of the uterus, ensuring it covers the wound from the posterior myoma. The interseed is positioned between the rectum and uterus to prevent adhesions with surrounding tissues, including the omentum. The serous layer of the uterus, which was injured, is also covered with the interseed.

The surgeon carefully opens the serous layer near the corneal region to remove a small fibroid adjacent to the fallopian tube. The fibroid, measuring approximately one centimeter, is extracted to prevent potential tubal blockage as it grows.

The surgeon carefully removes the myoma through a ten millimeter port, ensuring the tube is not damaged. Suction irrigation is performed, followed by port closure using a suture and facial closure needle to prevent future hernia formation. The rectus muscle and peritoneum are secured in one knot, and the port is tightly closed. A thorough examination confirms the surgical site is fine before the telescope is withdrawn.
